hints.Surge only: 'Vade' is also an imperative — both are commands: 'arise' and 'go'
hints.fecit only: 'Fecit' is a perfect indicative verb, not an imperative
hints.vade and fecit: 'Fecit' is not imperative — 'Surge' is also an imperative command

hints.to surge forward: While English 'surge' derives from this, 'surgere' in Latin means 'to rise/get up'
hints.to push: 'To push' is 'pellere' or 'urgere' — 'surgere' means 'to get up'

steps.reveal: Surge = Arise! / Get up! (2nd sg. imperative)

hints.present indicative: Present indicative 1st sg. would be 'surgo' — 'Surge' is a command
hints.present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'surgam/surgas' — 'Surge' is a direct command
hints.infinitive: Infinitive would be 'surgere' — 'Surge' is the imperative

hints.urgeo, urgere, ursi, —: These are the parts of 'urgere' (to press/urge)
hints.rego, regere, rexi, rectum: These are the parts of 'regere' (to rule/guide)
hints.sumo, sumere, sumpsi, sumptum: These are the parts of 'sumere' (to take)

hints.to save: 'To save' is 'salvare' — 'fecit' means 'has made' (he made you safe)

hints.present indicative: Present would be 'facit' — 'fecit' has the perfect stem 'fec-'
hints.perfect subjunctive: Perfect subjunctive would be 'fecerit' — 'fecit' lacks the subjunctive marker

hints.fido, fidere, fisus sum, —: These are the parts of 'fidere' (to trust) — a semi-deponent
hints.fugio, fugere, fugi, fugitum: These are the parts of 'fugere' (to flee)
hints.fero, ferre, tuli, latum: These are the parts of 'ferre' (to carry)

hints.accusative: Accusative would be 'fidem' — 'fides' is the nominative subject

steps.reveal: salvum = safe/well (acc. masc., predicate complement to 'te')

hints.nominative: Nominative would describe the subject — 'salvum' agrees with 'te' (accusative)
hints.ablative: Ablative would be 'salvo' — 'salvum' agrees with 'te' in the accusative
hints.adverb: 'Salvum' is an adjective, not an adverb — it modifies 'te' as an object complement

hints.nominative: 'Te' has no nominative form — nominative is 'tu'
hints.ablative: 'Te' can be ablative, but as direct object of 'fecit' it is accusative
hints.dative: Dative would be 'tibi' — 'te' is accusative here

translation: Arise, go; your faith has made you well
